sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 has a black background with two colored crosses (yellow upper-left, blue lower-center). A white dot at [45,36] sits at the blue cross center - likely the player. There are 8 gray 3x3 squares with colored 1px centers: 4 yellow-centered and 4 blue-centered. Purple bar at row 63 is likely a HUD element. Valid actions are UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT/SPACE.
- Goal model: Navigate the white dot to collect colored items and possibly deliver them to matching crosses.
- Action model: Arrow keys move the white dot; SPACE may interact. Need to probe.
- Plan: First probe movement to confirm the white dot is the player, then explore collecting items.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 26 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check what changed after the UP action. I need to compare the before and after frames to see if the white dot moved or if something else changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after the UP action
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation['nodes']
after = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']

# Find the white dot in before and after
print("=== BEFORE UP ===")
for n in before:
    if n['color'] == 'W':
        print(f"White node: id={n['id']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("=== AFTER UP ===")
for n in after:
    if n['color'] == 'W':
        print(f"White node: id={n['id']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Also check if any blue cross parts changed
print("\n=== Blue cross parts (after) ===")
for n in after:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] > 1:
        print(f"Blue node {n['id']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check animation changed bbox
print(f"\nAnimation changed bbox: {last_action_result['animation_changed_bbox']}")
print(f"Animation changed cell count: {last_action_result['animation_changed_cell_count']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
=== BEFORE UP ===
White node: id=20, boundary=[[45, 36]]
=== AFTER UP ===
White node: id=20, boundary=[[42, 36]]

=== Blue cross parts (after) ===
Blue node 16: pixels=13, boundary=[[29, 36], [41, 36]]
Blue node 19: pixels=13, boundary=[[42, 23], [42, 35]]
Blue node 21: pixels=13, boundary=[[42, 37], [42, 49]]
Blue node 22: pixels=13, boundary=[[43, 36], [55, 36]]

Animation changed bbox: [29, 23, 63, 63]
Animation changed cell count: 61

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The white dot moved from [45, 36] to [42, 36] - that's 3 pixels up. But more importantly, the ENTIRE blue cross moved up too! The blue cross center went from [45, 36] to [42, 36]. The white dot stays at the center of the blue cross.
So the white dot IS the player, and it moves the blue cross with it. The blue cross seems to be attached to the player.
Let me check: the blue cross parts before were at rows 32-44 (vertical bar), 45 (horizontal bar), 46-58 (vertical bar). After moving up, they're at rows 29-41, 42, 43-55. That's a shift of -3 rows.
So moving UP moves the player (white dot) and the blue cross up by 3 pixels. Let me verify this pattern.
Now the question is: what's the goal? The player controls the blue cross. There are colored items (gray squares with colored centers). Maybe the goal is to use the blue cross to "collect" or "capture" the blue-centered items?
Let me think about what SPACE might do. Maybe SPACE is used to interact with items.
Let me try moving the player to one of the blue-centered items and pressing SPACE.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated: The white dot at the blue cross center IS the player. Moving UP moved both the white dot AND the entire blue cross up by 3 pixels. The player controls the blue cross as a unit. Yellow cross and gray target squares remain stationary.
Goal model: Navigate the blue cross to collect the blue-centered target items (4 of them). SPACE may be needed to interact/collect.
Action model: Arrow keys move the blue cross + white dot together. Need to test SPACE for collecting.
Plan: Move the blue cross toward a blue-centered target and try SPACE to collect it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current position: white dot at [42, 36], blue cross centered there
# Blue-centered targets at: [16,48], [24,40], [24,53], [35,48]
# Let me move toward the closest blue target [24, 40]
# From center [42, 36] to [24, 40]: need to go UP and RIGHT
# Each UP moves 3 pixels up, so need about 6 UP moves to go from row 42 to row 24

# Let me first try one UP and one RIGHT to understand movement
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
